title: The Sticky Bat Controversy
story: Batter up! In 1983, baseball fans witnessed a wild scene. Kansas City's George Brett hit a game-winning home run against the Yankees. But wait! The umpires said Brett used too much pine tar on his bat. They took away the homer! Brett was so mad, he charged out of the dugout. Later, officials overturned the call. On July 24, they finished the game, and Brett's team won.
activity_question: What did the umpires initially say about George Brett's bat?
activity_choices: It was too much pine tar
It was borrowed from another player
activity_answer: It was too much pine tar
bonus_fact: Pine tar helps batters grip the bat better, but there are rules about how much they can use.

title: Fluffy Breakthrough: Ptarmigan Chick Hatches
story: A tiny, feathered miracle occurred in Ithaca, New York. For the first time ever, a ptarmigan chick hatched and grew up in captivity. This special bird, known for changing its feathers to blend with snow, made history in 1934. Scientists were thrilled to watch the fuzzy chick grow, learning more about these elusive arctic birds. It happened on July 24, marking a big step for wildlife research.
activity_question: What kind of bird hatched for the first time in captivity?
bonus_fact: Ptarmigans' feathers turn white in winter to hide from predators in the snow.

title: The Birth of an Art Nouveau Master
story: Brushes and paints were destined for greatness when Alphonse Mucha entered the world in 1860. Born on July 24 in a small town called Ivančice, this future art star would revolutionize the look of posters and paintings. Mucha's birthplace was then part of the Austrian Empire but is now in the Czech Republic. His flowing, nature-inspired style became the face of Art Nouveau, captivating people across Europe with its beauty.
activity_question: In what artistic style did Alphonse Mucha become famous?
bonus_fact: Mucha's art often featured elegant women surrounded by flowers and intricate patterns.

title: Gloria George: Trailblazing Indigenous Leader
story: Gloria George blazed a trail for Indigenous women in leadership. Born in Hubert, British Columbia in 1942, she grew up to become a powerful voice for her people. Her groundbreaking role opened doors for future generations.

title: Olympic Marathon Drama: A Twist at the Finish
story: The 1908 London Olympics saw an unforgettable marathon finish. Italian runner Dorando Pietri stumbled into the stadium first but collapsed repeatedly. Officials helped him cross the line, leading to his disqualification. American Johnny Hayes, who finished strong on his own, was declared the winner on July 24. His time of 2:55:18.4 set a new Olympic record.

He took a shortcut during the race
activity_answer: He received assistance before the finish line

story: Kenneth Bancroft Clark entered the world in 1914, destined to break barriers. Born on July 24 in the Panama Canal Zone, he grew up to become a powerful voice for civil rights. Clark's groundbreaking work, including his book Dark Ghetto, shed light on racial issues. His brilliance led him to make history as the first black president of the American Psychological Association.
activity_question: What important position did Kenneth Bancroft Clark hold?
activity_choices: First black president of the American Psychological Association
Governor of the Panama Canal Zone
Author of Panama Canal history books
Chief engineer of the Panama Canal
activity_answer: First black president of the American Psychological Association
follow_up_question: How might Clark's background have influenced his work on civil rights?

title: From Pilot to Vet: A Hero's Journey
story: James Eric Storrar soared into life in 1920. Born in Ormskirk, England on July 24, this future hero would fly high during World War II. Storrar bravely piloted missions in Operation Mosquito, even helping to destroy Hitler's 'Eagle's Nest'. After the war, he swooped into a new career as a veterinarian, caring for animals until 1995.
activity_question: What operation did James Eric Storrar participate in during World War II?
follow_up_question: How did Storrar's career change after the war?

title: Ska Guitar Star Born in Jamaica
story: Lynval Golding strummed his way into music history. Born in Saint Catherine, Jamaica on July 24, 1951, he became a guitar sensation. Golding later moved to England, where he rocked the new wave and ska scenes. He jammed with famous bands like The Specials and Fun Boy Three, bringing Caribbean rhythms to British beats.
activity_question: What type of music is Lynval Golding known for playing?
bonus_fact: Ska music combines elements of Caribbean mento and calypso with American jazz and rhythm and blues.

title: La Salle's Ill-Fated Journey to Louisiana
story: Brave explorer René-Robert Cavelier set sail from France on July 24, 1684. His mission? To start a new French colony in Louisiana. Four ships left the port of La Rochelle, filled with hopeful settlers. But the journey turned into a tough adventure. Storms, wrong turns, and other problems made things very difficult. In the end, only a few people survived the trip.
activity_question: What was the explorer's mission?
activity_choices: To start a new French colony in Louisiana
To discover a new sea route to India
To trade with Native American tribes
activity_answer: To start a new French colony in Louisiana
follow_up_question: Why do you think starting a new colony in a faraway land might be challenging?

title: Water Power Lights Up Grand Rapids
story: Sparks flew in Grand Rapids, Michigan as the world's first commercial hydroelectric power plant roared to life in 1880. On July 24, rushing river water spun giant turbines, creating electricity to light up the city. This groundbreaking technology harnessed nature's power, paving the way for cleaner energy and brighter cities across the globe.
activity_question: What natural resource did the power plant use to generate electricity?
follow_up_question: How might using water for electricity be better for the environment than burning coal?
